question.text: What happens to the escrow deposited by an acquirer if the acquirer fails to fulfill the open offer obligations?

answer.text: It is returned to the acquirer after deducting SEBI's administrative charges
It is distributed equally among all shareholders of the target company
It is held in trust until the acquirer makes a fresh offer within 6 months

explanation.text: **The Logic:** If the acquirer fails to fulfill the open offer obligations, the escrow amount is forfeited. This is the key enforcement mechanism ensuring acquirers have financial skin in the game. Conversely, if the acquirer completes all obligations, the escrow is released back. **The Trap:** "Distributed among shareholders" sounds fair but is not what happens — the escrow forfeiture serves as a penalty and deterrent, not as direct compensation to shareholders. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) tests escrow mechanics including forfeiture. Similar escrow forfeiture concepts exist in IPO regulations when issuers fail to meet listing timelines. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The forfeiture risk makes escrow structuring critical — the acquirer must weigh the cost of a bank guarantee premium against the opportunity cost of locking cash in escrow.
